title: Nested Conditionals
subtitle: Place if statements inside other if statements
learning_objectives: Write an if statement nested inside another if statement
Choose between nested conditionals and the and operator
Keep nested code readable by limiting nesting depth

sections.heading: if Inside an if: Nested Conditionals
sections.content:
A nested conditional is an if statement placed inside the block of another if statement. The inner if only runs when the outer condition is True. Each level of nesting adds another 4 spaces of indentation.

Use nested conditionals when the second check only makes sense after the first check has already passed.
sections.code_output:
Age check passed.
Welcome to the event!

sections.heading: Nested vs the and Operator
sections.content:
When both conditions must be True to run the same action, you can often simplify a nested conditional into a single if using and. Nested conditionals are better when you need different messages depending on which condition failed.

As a general rule: if your nesting goes deeper than two levels, look for ways to simplify — deeply nested code is hard to read and maintain.

exercises.title: Event Entry Check
exercises.prompt: Set age = 21 and has_ticket = False. Write a nested conditional: if age is 18 or above, check whether they have a ticket. Print the appropriate message for each case. Also handle the under-18 case. Note: The inner if/else lives inside the outer if block, so it needs 8 spaces of indentation (4 for the outer block + 4 more for the inner block).
exercises.starter_code:
age = 21
has_ticket = False

# Write your nested if/else below
hints: The inner if/else should be indented 4 more spaces than the outer if
has_ticket is already a boolean — you can write: if has_ticket:

tests.message: Age 21 without a ticket should print 'You need a ticket.'

exercises.title: Login Validator
exercises.prompt: Set username = "alice" and password = "secret". First check if the username is correct (equals "alice"). If so, check if the password is correct (equals "secret"). Print "Login successful", "Wrong password", or "Unknown user" as appropriate.
exercises.starter_code:
username = "alice"
password = "secret"

# Write your nested conditional below
hints: The password check is nested inside the username check

tests.message: Correct username and password should print 'Login successful'

quiz.question: What indentation does the inner if block use if the outer if block is at 4 spaces?
quiz.explanation: Each level of nesting adds 4 more spaces. If the outer if block uses 4-space indentation, the inner if block uses 8 spaces (4 + 4).

quiz.question: When is a nested conditional better than using the 'and' operator?
options: When you need to show different messages for each failing condition
When both conditions must be True
Nested conditionals are never better than and
quiz.explanation: Nested conditionals let you provide specific feedback about which condition failed. If you just need both conditions to be True and the response is the same either way, the 'and' operator is simpler.

quiz.question: How much indentation does the inner if block need if the outer if block uses 4 spaces?
quiz.explanation: Each level of nesting adds another 4 spaces. The outer if block is at 4 spaces. The inner if block is inside that, so it needs 8 spaces (4 + 4).
x = 10 is greater than 5 so we enter the outer if. y = 3 is not greater than 5 so the inner else runs, printing 'x big, y small'.

quiz.question: Why should you avoid nesting too many levels deep?
options: Python raises an error after 2 levels
It becomes hard to read and understand
quiz.explanation: Deeply nested code is hard to follow. More than 2 levels of nesting is a sign you should simplify — perhaps using 'and'/'or' operators or breaking the logic into separate steps.

completion_message: Great work! Nested conditionals give you fine-grained control over complex decisions. Remember to keep nesting shallow — two levels is usually the maximum before you should look for a simpler approach. Next lesson: combining conditions with logical operators.
